---
pagetitle: "jstats at Griffith"
title-prefix: ""
search: false
toc: true
toc-title: "On this page"
toc-location: right
format:
  html:
    include-in-header:
      text: |
        <meta name="robots" content="noindex, nofollow">
        <link rel="preconnect" href="https://fonts.googleapis.com">
        <link rel="preconnect" href="https://fonts.gstatic.com" crossorigin>
        <link href="https://fonts.googleapis.com/css2?family=Lato:wght@400;700&display=swap" rel="stylesheet">
---

<!--
  UNLISTED COURSE / COLLEAGUE FRONT DOOR.
  - Absent from the navbar and sidebar (not listed in _quarto.yml), so it never
    shows in a menu. Reachable only by URL:
    https://jma61.github.io/jstats-guides/griffith
  - search: false keeps it out of the site search; the robots noindex tag asks
    Google/Bing to skip it. Not locked down -- anyone with the URL can open it.
  - toc: true adds the right-hand "On this page" menu (the page's section
    headings). The bio heading is in a raw-HTML block, so it is not listed.

  HERO IMAGE: save the Griffith building photo into images/ as
  griffith-banner.jpg (the renamed copy is provided alongside this file).

  GRIFFITH RED: the title box uses #C8102E as a PLACEHOLDER -- one value, in the
  .gh-title rule below. Replace with the exact Griffith brand red when you have
  it.

  LAYOUT: the banner sits in the normal content column, so it is the same width
  as the text/tables below it -- full width on narrow windows, growing as the
  window widens, then capping and staying centered (Quarto's content column
  does this). To make the banner wider/narrower than the body text, wrap it in
  ::: {.column-page} (wider) or set a max-width on .griffith-hero. Navbar stays
  navy.
-->

```{=html}
<style>
  .griffith-hero {
    aspect-ratio: 4 / 1;
    min-height: 160px;
    background-image: url("images/griffith-banner.jpg");
    background-size: cover;
    background-position: center;
    display: flex;
    align-items: flex-end;
  }
  .gh-overlay { padding: 0 0 0.9rem 0; }
  .gh-label {
    display: block;
    width: fit-content;
    margin: 0;
    background: rgba(38, 38, 38, 0.88);
    color: #fff;
    font-family: "Lato Extended", Lato, "Helvetica Neue", Helvetica, Arial, sans-serif;
    font-weight: 700;
    font-size: 1.2rem;
    letter-spacing: 0.02em;
    padding: 0.28rem 0.8rem;
  }
  .gh-title {
    display: block;
    width: fit-content;
    margin: 0;
    background: #E51F30;            /* Griffith Canvas red */
    color: #fff;
    font-family: "Lato Extended", Lato, "Helvetica Neue", Helvetica, Arial, sans-serif;
    font-weight: 700;
    font-size: 2.05rem;            /* constant size -- boxes do not scale; only the photo does */
    line-height: 1.15;
    padding: 0.4rem 0.95rem;
  }
  /* THIS PAGE ONLY: make every section heading match the bio heading's smaller
     size. Scoped to the main content column so it hits the section headings and
     the bio name, but NOT the "On this page" menu title in the right margin. */
  #quarto-document-content h2 {
    font-size: 1.4rem;
  }
  /* THIS PAGE ONLY: push the "On this page" menu down so its header aligns with
     the top of the banner (the content column has top spacing the menu column
     lacks). Tweak this value if the header sits slightly above/below the banner. */
  #quarto-margin-sidebar {
    margin-top: 1.8rem;
  }
  /* THIS PAGE ONLY: keep the real site footer (so its width, height, and
     bottom-of-page position all stay native and correct), but swap its center
     text to the Griffith-specific disclaimer. The copyright on the left is the
     same on every page, so it is left untouched. */
  .nav-footer-center > * { display: none; }
  .nav-footer-center { font-size: 0; }
  .nav-footer-center::after {
    content: "jstats is an independent project that I develop and maintain in my own time. It is used in teaching at Griffith University but is not an official Griffith University publication. Views expressed and any errors are my own.";
    font-size: 0.875rem;
    line-height: 1.5;
  }
  /* THIS PAGE ONLY: hide the navbar's menu links so this landing page shows a
     clean branded bar -- logo and search only, no tabs. The body links in
     "Setting up" below are the only navigation a visitor needs here, and they
     open in new tabs so this page stays put behind them. The site-wide navbar
     is untouched on every other page (this style block loads only on this
     page). The target is the LEFT menu list (.navbar-nav.me-auto = Get Started /
     Guides / Reference / Books / Videos / Troubleshooting); the logo
     (.navbar-brand-container) and search (#quarto-search) are separate elements
     and stay visible. */
  .navbar-nav.me-auto { display: none !important; }
  /* OPTIONAL: for a barer bar, also hide the GitHub icon (right side) and the
     narrow-screen menu button. Uncomment both lines to enable. */
  /* .navbar-nav.ms-auto { display: none !important; } */
  /* .navbar-toggler      { display: none !important; } */
</style>
```

```{=html}
<div class="griffith-hero" role="img" aria-label="Griffith University building">
  <div class="gh-overlay">
    <span class="gh-label">jstats</span>
    <span class="gh-title">Statistics in R, Made Accessible</span>
  </div>
</div>
```

```{=html}
<div class="jbio">
  <div class="jbio-photo">
    <img src="images/jeff-headshot.jpg" alt="Jeff Ackerman">
  </div>
  <h2 class="jbio-name">About the developer: Jeff Ackerman</h2>
  <p>I'm a social scientist who teaches quantitative methods at Griffith University. I grew up in Pennsylvania, in the United States, and moved to Australia in 2013 to join Griffith; I'm now an Australian citizen. I studied statistics and research methods under several well-known methodologists, including C. R. Rao &mdash; himself a student of Ronald Fisher, the statistician who developed Analysis of Variance (ANOVA).</p>
  <p>I built jstats to make statistical analysis in R approachable for social-science researchers and students. R is powerful, but its learning curve and rough edges can make the move from commercial statistical software like SPSS, Stata, or SAS intimidating. jstats smooths that path: shorter, more consistent syntax; sensible defaults; protection from some of base R's more confusing behaviours; and output styled after the conventions the expensive commercial platforms use, so results look familiar from the first run.</p>
  <p>It stays close enough to base R that the skills you build transfer to the wider R world rather than locking you into a private dialect. jstats grew out of teaching statistics to social-science students who wanted to learn the methods, but not complex computer programming. Alongside statistics, I trained in computer programming at the university level decades ago, and that combination &mdash; now paired with modern AI-assisted software engineering and careful testing &mdash; is what makes it practical for one person to build and maintain a package this size. It's continuously being shaped by what students and colleagues run into when they put it to work.</p>
</div>
```

## Griffith CCJ Honours supervisors and other guests

Welcome, and thank you for taking a look at jstats. This site is a hands-on tour
of the tools your Honours and Masters students began using in my class -- though
jstats has been refined substantially since then. By following the short setup
steps on this site, you'll be ready to follow along during our Teams session on
Thursday 9 July, where I'll lead you through the Quick Start. By the end of the
hour, I expect you'll be able to open a dataset in R and run analyses including
linear regression, with no prior R experience needed. If you're not yet sure what
an R "package" is, the Quick Start will give you a feel for it. In short, a
package is an add-on toolset you install into R, and jstats is currently more
than 23,000 lines of computer code -- including its extensive help files --
working behind the scenes, so that the commands you're required to type stay
short and intuitive, and the output looks more like what you'd see in SPSS or
Stata. Please set up R, RStudio, and jstats before we meet (see "Setting up"
below). I look forward to seeing you on Teams at 10.00 am on Thursday 9 July.

## Students in 7036CCJ/CCJ736

This site is only for getting the software installed -- R, RStudio, and jstats
(see "Setting up" below). Your course materials live on the Canvas course site,
so before our first class please also finish the rest of the Welcome module and
Module 1 there. We'll go through the Quick Start together in that first online
session; if you'd like a preview, you're welcome to try the Quick Start on your
own, but don't be intimidated if the terms or output don't mean anything to you
yet. We'll cover all of that during the trimester.

## Setting up R, RStudio, and jstats

Work through these pages in order to get everything installed. Each one opens in
a new tab, so this page stays open behind it -- just switch back to this tab
whenever you like.

1. [**Install R and RStudio**](install-r-and-rstudio.qmd){target="_blank" rel="noopener"} -- two free downloads: the engine, then the dashboard
2. [**RStudio Orientation**](rstudio-orientation.qmd){target="_blank" rel="noopener"} -- a quick tour of the workspace
3. [**Install jstats**](install-jstats.qmd){target="_blank" rel="noopener"} -- adding the package itself
4. [**jstats Quick Start**](quickstart.qmd){target="_blank" rel="noopener"} -- run your first analysis *(optional)*

Already have R and RStudio? Skip straight to [**Install jstats**](install-jstats.qmd){target="_blank" rel="noopener"}.

The Quick Start is optional -- we'll go through it together in our Teams session,
so don't worry if anything isn't clear yet. If you hit a problem you can't get
past, have a look at [**Troubleshooting**](troubleshooting.qmd){target="_blank" rel="noopener"}
first, and we'll sort out anything that's left live.

## Using jstats in your own teaching?

You're welcome to. jstats is under continuous development -- I'm regularly adding
and refining functions. My current focus is the set of analyses I cover in my
course, but I plan to expand it with additional functions for other data-analysis
tasks common in social-science research and publishing. For now it installs from
GitHub, where the project lives during early development; before long I expect to
move it to CRAN -- R's central package archive -- so installation becomes simpler
and more routine. If you're using jstats in a course -- at Griffith or anywhere
else -- I'd be glad to hear about it, purely out of interest and so I can get a
sense of where the package is being used. Email me at
[SurveyCentre@griffith.edu.au](mailto:SurveyCentre@griffith.edu.au).
